options.label: B

options.label: C

question_text: Which of the following statements is/are correct?
question_text_latex: Which of the following statements is/are correct?

options.label: A
options.text: There exists a function f : [0,1] \to \mathbb{R} which is discontinuous at every point in [0,1] and |f(x)| is continuous at every point in [0,1]

options.label: B
options.text: Let F(x) = f(x) \cdot g(x). If f(x)is differentiable at x = a, f(a) = 0 and g(x)is continuous at x = a, then F(x)is always differentiable at x = a.

options.label: C
options.text: If f'(R) a = 2 and f'(L) a = 3, then f(x)is non-differentiable at x = a but will be always continuous at x = a.

options.label: D
options.text: If f(a) and f(b) possess opposite signs, then there must exist at least one solution of the equation f(x) = 0 in (a,b) provided f is continuous on [a,b].

options_latex.A: There exists a function f : [0,1] \to \mathbb{R} which is discontinuous at every point in [0,1] and |f(x)| is continuous at every point in [0,1]
options_latex.B: Let F(x) = f(x) \cdot g(x). If f(x)is differentiable at x = a, f(a) = 0 and g(x)is continuous at x = a, then F(x)is always differentiable at x = a.
options_latex.C: If f'(R) a = 2 and f'(L) a = 3, then f(x)is non-differentiable at x = a but will be always continuous at x = a.
options_latex.D: If f(a) and f(b) possess opposite signs, then there must exist at least one solution of the equation f(x) = 0 in (a,b) provided f is continuous on [a,b].
